documents.source: Bai Bureh and other leaders of the Temne people in Sierra Leone (in West Africa), letter to the British colonial authorities of Sierra Leone, 1896
documents.content:
“We received a letter from the colonial Governor sent to tell us that the British Queen now
takes the whole of Temne country. We read the Governor’s letter and we now know the law
that the Governor puts on us; namely, that we are to pay a tax on our houses each year so the
Government can build roads and a rail line.1 ... But we must inform you that we are not able to
pay a tax for our own houses because we have no power and no money to do so. So please tell
the Governor we beg him not to impose the tax and to consider the old agreement he made with
our fathers.”
raise money for transportation projects.

documents.source: William Douglas Mackenzie, Protestant minister born in South Africa who later emigrated to the United States, image from his illustrated book South Africa: Its History, Heroes and Wars, published in Chicago, 1899
documents.content:
The photograph shows two African employees of the Kimberley Diamond mines in South Africa.
The caption reads: “GOING HOME FROM THE MINES. These two Bechuanas [members of the
Tswana people of southern Africa] have been working at Kimberley or Johannesburg, have
received their pay in the golden coin of the British realm, and are traveling on their road from
100 to 400 miles to their distant home. They carry a few trophies purchased in the great city, and
reckon themselves as they approach their own town among the heroes and wise men of their
tribe.”

documents.source: Exchange of telegrams between William Low, British colonial official in Gold Coast (present-day Ghana), and British Colonial Secretary Joseph Chamberlain in London, 1900
documents.content:
“[April 30, 1900; Low to Chamberlain]: Update on the fighting against the Ashanti1 — Ashanti
warriors launched a serious attack on a telegraph clerk while he was repairing telegraph lines
that had previously been severed by the Ashanti. Other telegraph clerks report Ashanti troops
have effectively blockaded the main road to the city of Kumasi since April 25th. Government
troops from Lagos [in Nigeria] were sent to Kumasi. We hope that they will succeed in
reopening communications.
[May 1, 1900; Chamberlain to Low]: In response to your telegram of April 30th, we will be
sending 250 more troops from southern Nigeria to Kumasi to counter Ashanti attacks. I will also
send 50 additional Frontier Police from Sierra Leone to the Ashanti territories for protection of
British life and property.”
wars of resistance against British colonial expansion during the nineteenth century.

documents.source: Peninsular and Oriental Steam Navigation Company (P&O), a British shipping company that provided passenger and freight service from Great Britain to many parts of the world, an advertising brochure for travelers on P&O’s ships to Egypt, 1908
documents.content:
“As we leave the ship at Alexandria and board the train to Cairo, we may observe simple farm
and rural scenes but also, here and there, the huge new cotton mills that remind us that Egypt is
progressive and up to date.
Cairo has changed greatly since 1882,1 and it is inevitable that, in a country so closely in touch
with Western civilization, many traditional local shops have been replaced by storefronts with
large windows displaying products of European industry. In the west end of Cairo huge hotels
and commercial buildings have replaced the former delightful residences of the elite. In the
hotels, the vibrant social life of modern Cairo is in full swing, making the city attractive to
many.”

documents.source: British Parliamentary Commission appointed to investigate the spread of tuberculosis in South Africa, report of its findings presented to the British Parliament, 1914 “In those towns which were the first to be opened up by the railway, the disease of tuberculosis has prevailed the longest and to the greatest extent
documents.content:
The opening up of South Africa as a result of the development of diamond and gold mining has
led to the creation of labor centers and the clustering of the African and mixed-race populations
in towns and in separate workers’ quarters, where the conditions of housing and general health
have been bad in the extreme. For the first time, increasing numbers of Africans are crowding
into urban centers in search of work that dire economic conditions and expanding needs are
forcing them to undertake.
With these changes have come changes in habits, in clothing, and in diet, the adoption of
European vices, and exposure to unhealthy conditions of labor in mines and elsewhere.... It is
evident that the rise of industry has produced exactly those conditions that can best account for
the spread of tuberculosis in South Africa.”

documents.source: William Claridge and Hugh Clifford, British colonial officials employed in the colony of Gold Coast (present-day Ghana), A History of the Gold Coast and Ashanti, book published in London, 1915 “
documents.content:
The completion of the railway from [the port] of Sekondi to Kumasi in 1903 has been followed
by the construction of another line from [the port of] Accra to the cocoa-bearing districts, and
these two lines have enormously facilitated trade. The first line has also been responsible for
the establishment of numerous European trading firms in Kumasi, which has become a great
trading center.
The abolition of the slave trade and the acquisition of most of the gold-bearing lands in the
colony by European entrepreneurs has caused the local people to seek work in timber, rubber
collecting, and agriculture, which the Government has encouraged by holding agricultural
shows and providing traveling instructors to assist farmers with expert advice.
A submarine cable to England was connected in July 1886, and telegraphic communication
between different parts of the country has been extended even to the northern districts.”

documents.source: Toyin Falola, Nigerian historian and public intellectual, memoir about his childhood in the 1950s and 1960s, published in 2004 “
documents.content:
The trains on which I sometimes took joy rides after skipping school were part of the changes
that the British had introduced to Nigeria before I was born. By the 1930s, the new railway
system had connected Ibadan, my home city, to the rest of the country.
Ibadan was in the heartland of the cocoa-growing belt. Millions of cocoa bags were brought to
the city, to be carried by train to the port in Lagos for onward transmission to Europe. Ibadan
was also in the region producing palm oil and peanuts, which ended up in far-flung places such
as New York and London....
The trains brought new settlers to Ibadan. They created new neighborhoods defined by
ethnicity, race, and education. The old city, whose native residents called each other ‘Mesiogo,’
had remained intact, but the new immigrants had established their own new city, which had
become fully developed, well populated, and vibrant. The Mesiogo would travel from the old to
the new city to shop, buying goods from the Lebanese, Syrians, and Indians as well as from the
Igbo, Edo, Urhobo, and Ibibio immigrants from other parts of Nigeria who had flocked to the
city. The new areas were the first to acquire the modern amenities of electricity and pipe-borne
water.”

parts.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which new transportation and/or communication technologies affected
African societies during the period circa 1850 to 1960.
parts.scoring_notes: Full rubric: Thesis (1), Contextualization (1), Evidence from Documents (2), Evidence Beyond Documents (1), Sourcing (1), Complexity (1). Total: 7 points.

scoring_points.description: Thesis: Historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning about the extent to which new transportation and/or communication technologies affected African societies circa 1850–1960.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Responds to the prompt with a historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning about the extent to which new transportation and/or communication technologies affected African societies during the period circa 1850 to 1960. The thesis must either provide some indication of the reason for making that claim OR establish categories of the argument. Does not earn the point if the response only restates the prompt, provides an overgeneralized statement, or states a claim that is not historically defensible.

sample_responses.response: New transportation and communication technologies increased trade and contacts between African societies and other world regions, but they also served as tools of European colonial domination that extracted African resources and labor while undermining traditional African political structures.
sample_responses.commentary: Establishes a line of reasoning that evaluates the topic of the prompt with analytic categories — both the extent of economic integration and the colonial exploitation dimension. This is a minimally acceptable complex thesis.

sample_responses.response: Europeans used new technologies like railways, canals, and telegraphs to exercise more direct control over their colonial empires in Africa and to extract more natural resources and raw materials for their industrialized economies.
sample_responses.commentary: Establishes a line of reasoning that evaluates the topic of the prompt with analytic categories — colonial control and resource extraction as the defining effect of technology on African societies.

sample_responses.response: New communications technologies affected African societies.
sample_responses.commentary: Restatement of the prompt. Does not establish a line of reasoning or provide any indication of the reason for the claim.

required_elements: Historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning.
alternatives.correct_example: Railways and telegraphs helped Europeans control Africa.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.

scoring_points.description: Contextualization: Describe a broader historical context relevant to transportation/communication technologies and African societies circa 1850–1960.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Accurately describe a context relevant to transportation or communication technologies and African societies during the period circa 1850 to 1960. Must be more than a phrase or reference. Acceptable broader contexts include: First Industrial Revolution, Second Industrial Revolution, imperialism and colonialism across Africa and Asia, the Transatlantic Slave Trade, the 'civilizing mission'/White Man's Burden/Social Darwinism, anti-colonial resistance movements, Scramble for Africa/1885 Berlin Conference, decolonization, World Wars I and II, migration of laborers from Asia to Africa.

sample_responses.response: European attitudes such as the civilizing mission and Social Darwinism contributed to efforts by some colonial states to build railways and spread new communication technologies in Africa, as European powers justified colonial rule by claiming they were modernizing and uplifting African peoples.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately describes the civilizing mission ideology as a broader historical context relevant to why European colonial states built transportation and communication technologies in Africa.

sample_responses.response: Industrialization in Europe contributed to the spread of new technologies to Africa.
sample_responses.commentary: Minimally acceptable contextualization — identifies industrialization as the broader context that made new technologies available and that colonial powers then spread to Africa.

sample_responses.response: Many Africans adopted Islam through the influence of land-based empires and commercial networks.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not provide context relevant to the topic of the prompt (transportation/communication technologies in Africa).

required_elements: Describe a broader historical context relevant to the prompt.
alternatives.correct_example: The Scramble for Africa and the 1885 Berlin Conference accelerated European colonial expansion into the African interior, creating demand for railways and telegraphs to administer and extract resources from vast colonial territories.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.

scoring_points.description: Evidence from Docs: Use content of at least three documents to address the topic of the prompt.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Accurately describe — rather than simply quote — the content from at least three of the documents to address the topic of new transportation and/or communication technologies and African societies during the period circa 1850 to 1960.

sample_responses.response: Document 4 describes how Egypt built European-style cotton mills, hotels, and storefronts, showing how railways and modern infrastructure transformed Egyptian urban life and economy. Document 2 shows South African miners at the Kimberley Diamond Mine traveled up to 400 miles between their homes and the mine, demonstrating how road and transportation networks connected workers to colonial mining centers. Document 7 explains that the railroad connected Ibadan, Nigeria to the rest of the country and enabled millions of cocoa bags to be carried to the port in Lagos for export, showing how railways integrated African agricultural regions into the global economy.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately describes the content of three documents to address the topic of transportation/communication technologies and African societies. Describes rather than merely quoting document content.

required_elements: Use content of at least three documents to address the topic of the prompt.
alternatives.correct_example: Document 3 shows Ashanti warriors attacked telegraph lines and blockaded roads, showing that Africans knew how important transportation and communication technologies were for maintaining Britain's control of her colonies.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.

scoring_points.description: Evidence from Docs+: Support an argument in response to the prompt using at least four documents.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Support an argument in response to the prompt by accurately using the content of at least four documents. The four documents do not have to be used in support of a single argument — they can be used across sub-arguments or to address counterarguments.

sample_responses.response: Document 3 shows that Ashanti warriors attacked telegraph lines and blockaded roads specifically to disrupt British communications — demonstrating that Africans understood and actively resisted the role of transportation and communication technologies in European colonial control. Similarly, Document 1 shows that Temne leaders in Sierra Leone protested British taxes intended to build roads and a railway, revealing African awareness that colonial infrastructure projects served European interests and were financed by African labor. By contrast, Documents 6 and 7 show how railways in the Gold Coast and Nigeria facilitated the export of cocoa and other agricultural products, integrating African economies into global markets in ways that benefited European trading firms but also transformed local African economic activity.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses four documents (1, 3, 6, 7) to support an argument about how transportation technologies both enabled colonial control (Documents 1, 3) and economic integration (Documents 6, 7), demonstrating the multi-dimensional effects on African societies.

required_elements: Support an argument using at least four documents.
alternatives.correct_example: Document 5's findings show that the introduction of the railway significantly affected South African society by leading to the spread of disease and the division of native and mixed-race groups along racial and ethnic lines.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Beyond: Use at least one additional piece of specific historical evidence beyond the documents relevant to an argument.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Must use at least one specific piece of historical evidence relevant to the extent to which African societies changed in response to transportation/communication technologies during the period circa 1850 to 1960. The evidence must be different from the evidence used to earn the contextualization point and must be more than a phrase or reference. Acceptable beyond-document evidence includes: the Scramble for Africa/1885 Berlin Conference; end of the Transatlantic Slave Trade; Indian and other indentured laborers; African participation in World War I; the Zulu Rebellion against British colonial rule; political and economic modernization in Egypt; apartheid in South Africa; 19th-20th century technologies including the Maxim gun, quinine, airplanes, Suez Canal; atrocities in Belgian Congo and Namibia connected to resource extraction; Cecil Rhodes' activities such as the Cape to Cairo Railway or DeBeers Mining; specific European colonies not in documents.

sample_responses.response: The extraction of natural resources in Africa often led to abuses against native populations such as in the Belgian Congo where the local people were forced to collect rubber and were punished severely if they failed to meet the production quotas — demonstrating how transportation infrastructure (river steamboats, roads) enabled the systematic exploitation of African labor and resources.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses the Belgian Congo rubber atrocities as specific evidence beyond the documents relevant to an argument about how transportation technologies enabled colonial resource extraction and its harmful effects on African societies.

sample_responses.response: Europeans constructed railways across parts of Africa to strengthen their direct military rule, such as the Cape to Cairo railway, which the British used to extend their control from Southern Africa northward.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses the Cape to Cairo railway project as specific evidence beyond the documents, relevant to an argument about how transportation technologies served colonial control objectives.

required_elements: Use at least one additional piece of specific historical evidence beyond the documents.
alternatives.correct_example: The Suez Canal, completed in 1869, dramatically reduced travel time between Europe and Asia and transformed Egypt into a strategic chokepoint that European powers competed to control, demonstrating how transportation technologies reshaped the political geography of Africa.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.

scoring_points.description: Sourcing (HAPP): Explain how/why the point of view, purpose, historical situation, or audience of at least two documents is relevant to an argument.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Must explain how or why — rather than simply identifying — the document's point of view, purpose, historical situation, or audience is relevant to an argument that addresses the prompt for each of the two documents sourced. Identifying HAPP without explaining its relevance to an argument does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Document 1's purpose was to convince the colonial government of Sierra Leone not to tax its subjects beyond what they are able to pay — the Temne authors are careful with their language because they want to emphasize that they are loyal subjects to the empire while still being firm in stating that they cannot pay the new house tax. This caution in tone reflects how colonized peoples had to frame their resistance in terms acceptable to colonial authorities, which helps explain why African resistance to transportation infrastructure projects is underrepresented in colonial records compared to European accounts.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains the purpose and POV of Document 1 as relevant to an argument about African resistance to colonial infrastructure — connecting the caution of the tone to patterns of colonial record-keeping that may distort our understanding.

sample_responses.response: Document 4 is an advertising brochure from a British shipping company promoting tourism to Egypt — its purpose is to highlight for Western tourists the progress and modern amenities Egypt has achieved under European influence, so it presents railways, cotton mills, and modern hotels entirely positively, without acknowledging any negative effects on Egyptian society or displaced traditional communities.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains the purpose of Document 4 (advertising brochure for a British shipping company targeting Western tourists) as relevant to an argument about why the document presents a one-sided, pro-colonial view of technology's impact on Egypt.

required_elements: Explain how/why the point of view, purpose, historical situation, or audience of at least two documents is relevant to an argument.
alternatives.correct_example: Document 2 was written for a Western, British audience who would likely have interpreted the photograph as evidence of the economic benefits that British colonial rule was bringing to African workers — the audience shapes why the caption presents the workers positively as 'heroes and wise men of their tribe' rather than depicting the exploitative conditions of the diamond mines.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.

scoring_points.description: Complexity: Demonstrate a complex understanding of the extent to which transportation/communication technologies affected African societies circa 1850–1960.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Demonstrates a complex understanding of the historical development through sophisticated argumentation and/or effective use of evidence. May demonstrate complexity through: explaining multiple themes or perspectives; explaining multiple causes or effects, similarities or differences, or continuities or changes; explaining both cause and effect/similarity and difference; explaining relevant connections within and across periods or areas; effectively using seven documents; explaining HAPP of at least four documents; using documents and beyond-doc evidence to demonstrate sophisticated understanding of different perspectives.

sample_responses.response: The documents collectively show that Westerners typically portrayed advances in transportation and communications as bringing civilization and progress to Africa (Documents 2, 4, 6), while in reality such advances were often used to increase the oppression of colonized societies or facilitate suppression of indigenous revolts (Documents 1, 3), or had unintended negative consequences acknowledged even by colonial authorities themselves (Document 5). This contrast in perspectives — revealed through careful sourcing analysis — demonstrates that the 'progress' narrative in colonial documents systematically obscured the extractive and coercive nature of infrastructure development in Africa.
sample_responses.commentary: Demonstrates complex understanding by explaining how point of view, purpose, and historical situation of at least four documents (2, 4, 6, 1, 3, 5) supports a sophisticated argument about the gap between colonial narratives of technological progress and the reality of colonial exploitation.

required_elements: Demonstrate a complex understanding through sophisticated argumentation and/or effective use of evidence.
alternatives.correct_example: The extent to which technologies affected African societies depended fundamentally on who controlled them: when Africans controlled access to roads and telegraphs (even temporarily, as in the Ashanti's blockade of Kumasi in Document 3), these technologies served African resistance; when Europeans controlled them, they served colonial extraction and domination.

wrong_examples: A response that fails to address this specific criterion.
A vague or overgeneralized response that does not meet the standard.
